request.question: What acress starred in both "Third Person" and "From Here to Eternity"?

title: Grammatical person
Direct–inverse language
Third Person (band)
Kim Basinger
Du-reformen
Third Person (film)
Pitcairn's Island (novel)
Note verbale
Shinji Mikami
Appears
sentences: Grammatical person, in linguistics, is the grammatical distinction between deictic references to participant(s) in an event; typically the distinction is between the speaker (first person), the addressee (second person), and others (third person).

Put in simple colloquial English, first person is that which includes the speaker, namely, "I," "we," "me," and "us," second person is the person or people to whom are spoken, literally, "you," and third person includes all that is not listed above.
Grammatical person typically defines a language's set of personal pronouns.
It also frequently affects verbs, and sometimes nouns or possessive relationships.

The definition of a direct–inverse language is a matter under research, but it is widely understood to involve different grammar for transitive predications according to the relative positions of their "subject" and their "object" on a "person hierarchy", which, in turn, is some combination of saliency and animacy specific to a given language.
The direct construction is the unmarked one.
The direct construction is used when the subject of the transitive clause outranks the object in the person hierarchy, and the inverse is used when the object outranks the subject.
The existence of direct–inverse morphosyntax is usually accompanied by proximate–obviative morphosyntax.
The direct–inverse dimension subsumes the proximate–obviative dimension.

Across languages, obviation almost always involves the third person (although second-person obviation is reported for some Nilo-Saharan languages ), and the direct–inverse alternation is usually presented as being a way of marking the proximate–obviative distinction between two (or more) third person arguments of a sentence.

However, there are at least two languages with inverse systems, the Mesoamerican languages Zoque and Huastec, in which inverse morphosyntax is never used when both subject and object are third person, but only when one of these arguments is third person and the other is a speech act participant (SAP), the first or second person .
Third Person was an improvising trio formed in 1990 in New York City, led by cellist Tom Cora and drummer Samm Bennett.
Each performance featured an invited guest: A third person.
Guests included guitarist Marc Ribot, harpist Zeena Parkins, clarinet player Don Byron and saxophone players George Cartwright and Kazutoki Umezu and many others.
Kimila Ann "Kim" Basinger ( ; born December 8, 1953) is an American actress, singer and former fashion model.
Following a successful modeling career in New York during the 1970s, Basinger moved to Los Angeles where she began her acting career on television in 1976.
She starred in several made-for-TV films, including a remake of "From Here to Eternity" (1979), before making her feature debut in the 1981 drama "Hard Country".
She won the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress for her performance as Lynn Bracken in the 1997 film "L.A. Confidential".
Du-reformen (] , "the you-reform") was the process in the late 1960s of popularization of the second-person singular pronoun "du" as a universal form of address in Sweden.

The use of "du" (cognate with English "thou", French "tu", and German "du") replaced an intricate former system where people chiefly addressed each other in third person, with or without a preceding Mr./Mrs./Ms. "(herr, fru "or" fröken)" before the title, often omitting both surname and given name.

Less respectfully, people could be addressed with Mr./Mrs./Ms. plus surname, or in a family setting, alternatively even less respectfully, with the plain name or third person pronoun "han" ("he") or "hon" (she).

Third Person is a 2013 British-German-American romance film directed and written by Paul Haggis and starring an ensemble cast consisting of Liam Neeson, Mila Kunis, Adrien Brody, Olivia Wilde, James Franco, Moran Atias, Kim Basinger, and Maria Bello.
The film premiered at the 2013 Toronto Film Festival.
Pitcairn's Island is the third installment in the fictional trilogy by Charles Nordhoff and James Norman Hall about the mutiny aboard HMS "Bounty".
It is preceded by "Mutiny on the "Bounty"" and "Men Against the Sea".
The novel first appeared in "The Saturday Evening Post" (from 22 September 1934 through 3 November 1934) then was published in 1934 by Little, Brown and Company.
Chapters I–XV are told in the third person, and Chapters XVI–XXI are told in the first person by John Adams.
The epilogue that follows is in the third person.

Note verbale (] , verbal note) is a piece of diplomatic correspondence prepared in the third person and unsigned: less formal than a note (also called a letter of protest) but more formal than an aide-mémoire.
A note verbale can also be referred to as a third person note (TPN).
Shinji Mikami (Japanese: 三上 真司 , Hepburn: Mikami Shinji , born August 11, 1965) is a Japanese video game director and producer.
Starting his career at Capcom in 1990, he went on to direct many of the company's biggest titles.
He directed the first installment of the survival horror series "Resident Evil" in 1996 and the first installment of "Dino Crisis" in 1999.
He returned to Resident Evil to direct the remake of the first game in 2002.
He returned to direct third person shooter "Resident Evil 4" in 2005.
The last game he directed at Capcom was 2006's God Hand, a third person brawler.
He then worked with PlatinumGames to direct the third person shooter "Vanquish" in 2010.
After that he started his own studio called Tango Gameworks.
Under that company he directed the third person horror game "The Evil Within".
The company has since been acquired by the American company ZeniMax Media.
"Appears" is a song recorded by Japanese recording artist Ayumi Hamasaki.
It was released by Avex Trax on November 10, 1999 as the sixth single from her second studio album "Loveppears" (1999), which was released on the same day.
Alongside this, it has been released in several other territories with different release dates under her Western alias Ayu.
It also served as Hamasaki's first limited edition single, limiting physical sales to 300,000 copies.
The track was written by Hamasaki herself, while production was handled by long-time collaborator Max Matsuura.
Musically, "Appears" is a dance song written in third person perspective, and is about the third person watching a happy and loving relationship.
Upon its release, "Appears" received positive reviews from music critics.
Alexey Eremenko, writing for AllMusic, selected the track as the best song from the album and her career.
However, an editor from "CD Journal" criticized the amount of remixes on the CD single.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: What acress starred in both "Third Person" and "From Here to Eternity"?
hop1_docs: From Here to Eternity (Envy album) | From Here To Eternity is the second album by the band Envy.  It was released in Japan in 1998 by the label H.G. Fact in both CD and LP formats.  As has been noted by reviewers, this album shows a logical progression from the previous one, and indicates to the listener which direction the band will take their sound in the future.

From Here to Eternity: Live | From Here to Eternity: Live is a live album by English punk rock band The Clash.  It was released on 4 October 1999 through Epic Records.  The songs were recorded at different shows.  Some of the recordings featured also appear in the film "Rude Boy".  "London's Burning", "What's My Name" and "I Fought the Law" were instrumentally overdubbed to repair some technical deficiencies of the original live recording.
From Here to Eternity (disambiguation) | From Here to Eternity is a 1953 film.
From Here to Eternity (Giorgio Moroder song) | "From Here to Eternity" is a song by songwriter, producer and performer Giorgio Moroder, released in 1977.

From Here to Eternity (miniseries) | From Here to Eternity is a 1979 American three-part, six-hour television miniseries and a remake of the 1953 film "From Here to Eternity" based on the 1951 novel of the same name.  All three conclude with the December 7, 1941 attack on Pearl Harbor.  The miniseries originally aired in three two-hour installments on three consecutive Wednesdays on NBC on February 14, 21 and 28, 1979.
From Here to Eternity (Giorgio Moroder album) | From Here to Eternity is a 1977 album composed, produced and performed by Italian musician Giorgio Moroder.

From Here to Eternity (Iron Maiden song) | "From Here to Eternity" is a song by English heavy metal band Iron Maiden, released as the second single from their album "Fear of the Dark", released in 1992.
hop1_titles: From Here to Eternity (Giorgio Moroder song)
From Here to Eternity (Giorgio Moroder album)
From Here to Eternity (Iron Maiden song)
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - "From Here to Eternity" appears in the passages: [3] From Here to Eternity (disambiguation) — "From Here to Eternity is a 1953 film." and [5] From Here to Eternity (miniseries) — "1979 American three-part, six-hour television miniseries" (both quoted tokens from those passages).
  - "Third Person": no matching passage or title present in the provided passages (no quotes available).
- Ambiguities:
  - "From Here to Eternity" has multiple candidate pages in the passages: "From Here to Eternity (disambiguation)" and "From Here to Eternity (miniseries)"; the 1953 film is referenced by the disambiguation but no dedicated 1953 film page content is included here.
  - "Third Person" is missing entirely from the passages and could refer to multiple works (film, book, etc.); no candidate pages are listed.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity (as described in these passages): "an overlapping cast member (actress) who appears in both 'Third Person' and 'From Here to Eternity'." The precise missing fact: the full name of the actress who starred in both works (not present in the passages).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized next hops):
  1) Open "Third Person (film)" page (or "Third Person (2013 film)") — check the lede/infobox and the "Cast" section for credited actresses. Search keywords: "Third Person film cast" / "Third Person (film) cast list".
  2) Open "From Here to Eternity (1953 film)" page — check lede/infobox and "Cast" section for actresses. If that page is not separate, open "From Here to Eternity (disambiguation)" then the 1953 film page. Search keywords: "From Here to Eternity 1953 cast", "From Here to Eternity cast Deborah Kerr".
  3) Open "From Here to Eternity (miniseries)" page — check its "Cast" section in case the miniseries has a different overlapping actress.
  4) Compare the cast lists from the above pages to identify any identical actress name(s) appearing in both.
Final answer deferred until those pages and cast lists are retrieved.
response.hop2_query: Which actress starred in both "Third Person" and "From Here to Eternity"?
hop2_docs: From Here to Eternity (Envy album) | From Here To Eternity is the second album by the band Envy.  It was released in Japan in 1998 by the label H.G. Fact in both CD and LP formats.  As has been noted by reviewers, this album shows a logical progression from the previous one, and indicates to the listener which direction the band will take their sound in the future.

From Here to Eternity (novel) | From Here to Eternity is the debut novel of American author James Jones, published by Scribner's in 1951.  Set in 1941, the novel focuses on several members of a U.S. Army infantry company stationed in Hawaii in the months leading up to the Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor.  It is loosely based on Jones' experiences in the pre-World War II Hawaiian Division's 27th Infantry and the unit in which he served, Company E ("The Boxing Company").  Fellow company member Hal Gould said that while the novel was based on the company, including some depictions of actual persons, the characters are fictional, and the harsh conditions and described events are inventions.

Underworld: The Eternal War | Underworld: The Eternal War (also known as Underworld: The Game) is a 2004 third-person shooter video game based on the vampire film "Underworld" which was released in 2003.
From Here to Eternity (disambiguation) | From Here to Eternity is a 1953 film.

Eternal Summer (soundtrack) | Eternal Summer Original Soundtrack () is the soundtrack album for the 2006 Taiwanese film "Eternal Summer", starring Joseph Chang, Bryant Chang and Kate Yeung; and directed by Leste Chen.  It was released on 12 December 2006 by Rock Records.  The album features 23 musical scores.  Three editions were released including the Preorder Edition and Regular Edition of which both include a CD and a VCD.  Both of which contains "Eternal Summer" sung by Mayday and other songs by Fish Leong and Mayday.
From Here to Eternity (Giorgio Moroder song) | "From Here to Eternity" is a song by songwriter, producer and performer Giorgio Moroder, released in 1977.

From Here to Eternity (miniseries) | From Here to Eternity is a 1979 American three-part, six-hour television miniseries and a remake of the 1953 film "From Here to Eternity" based on the 1951 novel of the same name.  All three conclude with the December 7, 1941 attack on Pearl Harbor.  The miniseries originally aired in three two-hour installments on three consecutive Wednesdays on NBC on February 14, 21 and 28, 1979.
